# MiniMax Sparse Attention (Jun 2026)
## AI Paper Slop
4050 subscribers
2 likes

### Description
69 views
Posted: 14 Jun 2026
Title: MiniMax Sparse Attention (Jun 2026)
Link: 
Date: June 2026

Summary:
This paper introduces MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a blockwise sparse attention mechanism built upon Grouped Query Attention (GQA). MSA employs a lightweight Index Branch to score and select a subset of key-value blocks for each GQA group, allowing the Main Branch to perform exact block-sparse attention. By co-designing the algorithm with specialized GPU kernels, MSA achieves significant wall-clock speedups—up to 14.2× for prefill and 7.6× for decoding at 1M context—while maintaining the performance levels of standard GQA on large-scale multimodal models. [...] Mini Max sparse attention by the team at Mini Max.. We are jumping right into it today because this paper solves the absolute quadratic nightmare of running ultra long context windows at actual deployment scale. Oh, yeah, it's total game changer. Right. So the core insight here for you listening is that they build a block wise, sparse attention mechanism directly on top of grouped query attention or GQA. And they use this really lightweight, entirely exp- free index branch to retrieve only the top K key value blocks per GQA group. Yeah. And then the main branch executes a KV outer sparse attention kernel. Exactly. And the hardware level wall clock speedups are just staggering. Like on an H800 GPU, at a 1 million token context on a 109 billion parameter mixture of experts model, which is [...] a 109 billion parameter mixture of experts model, which is massive. Totally massive. Yeah. This architecture delivers a 28.4 X reduction in attention, FLOPs, because of 14.2 X faster pre-fill and a 7.6 X decoding wall clock speedup. Wow. And it does all of that while completely matching the performance of a dense GQA baseline across, you know, text math code and multimodal benchmark. It's honestly a massive technical flex. I mean, for everyone listening, you know that everybody wants a million token context right now. You need it for agent work flows, repository scale coding, all of that. but standard softmax attention just melts the GPUs. It literally melts them. Yeah. So the mission of this deep dive today on AI Paper Slop is to unpack exactly how Mini Max took this theoretical sparsity

results.title: MiniMax Sparse Attention | Papers | HyperAI
results.content:
## One-sentence Summary

MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) is a Grouped Query Attention variant that employs a lightweight Index Branch to independently select Top-k key-value blocks per group, enabling exact block-sparse computation that matches standard performance while reducing per-token compute by 28.4x at one million tokens and delivering 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding speedups on H800 GPUs through a co-designed kernel leveraging exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention.

## Key Contributions

## Introduction [...] ## One-sentence Summary

MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) is a Grouped Query Attention variant that employs a lightweight Index Branch to independently select Top-k key-value blocks per group, enabling exact block-sparse computation that matches standard performance while reducing per-token compute by 28.4x at one million tokens and delivering 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding speedups on H800 GPUs through a co-designed kernel leveraging exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention.

## Key Contributions

## Introduction [...] Ultra-long-context capability is becoming indispensable for frontier LLMs: agentic workflows, repository-scale code reasoning, and persistent memory all require the model to jointly attend over hundreds of thousands to millions of tokens, yet the quadratic cost of softmax attention makes this untenable at deployment scale. We introduce MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a blockwise sparse attention built upon Grouped Query Attention (GQA). A lightweight Index Branch scores key-value blocks and independently selects a Top-k subset for each GQA group, enabling group-specific sparse retrieval while maintaining efficient block-level execution; the Main Branch then performs exact block-sparse attention over only the selected blocks. Designed around a principle of simplicity and scalability, MSA

We introduce MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a blockwise sparse attention built upon Grouped Query Attention (GQA). A lightweight Index Branch scores key-value blocks and independently selects a Top-k subset for each GQA group, enabling group-specific sparse retrieval while maintaining efficient block-level execution; the Main Branch then performs exact block-sparse attention over only the selected blocks. Designed around a principle of simplicity and scalability, MSA [...] Hugging Face's logo

# MiniMax Sparse Attention

MiniMaxAI

## Abstract

MiniMax Sparse Attention enables efficient processing of ultra-long contexts in large language models through blockwise sparsity and optimized GPU execution, achieving significant speedups while maintaining performance. [...] around a principle of simplicity and scalability, MSA is deliberately streamlined, making it straightforward to deploy efficiently across a broad range of GPUs. To translate sparsity into practical speedups, we co-design MSA with a GPU execution path that uses exp-free Top-k selection and KV-outer sparse attention to improve tensor-core utilization under block-granular access. On a 109B-parameter model with native multimodal training, MSA performs on par with GQA while reducing per-token attention compute by 28.4x at 1M context. Paired with our co-designed kernel, MSA achieves 14.2x prefill and 7.6x decoding wall-clock speedups on H800. Our inference kernel is available at:  A production-grade natively multimodal model powered by MSA has been publicly released at:

results.title: MiniMax Sparse Attention - arXiv
results.content:
## 3 MSA

We introduce MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA), a GQA-based sparse attention mechanism with two branches, as illustrated in Figure 1. For each query token, a lightweight Index Branch selects a small set of key blocks from the causal context, and the Main Branch computes softmax attention over the tokens in those blocks. The Index Branch adds only two projection matrices to standard GQA, operates at block granularity, and makes selections independently for each GQA group. We describe the architecture in Section 3.1 and the training procedure in Section 3.2.

### 3.1 Architecture [...] ### 2.2 Sparse Attention as a Two-Stage Process

A sparse attention layer factors causal attention into an indexer that selects which keys to attend to and a sparse attention computation over the selected keys. For each query position ,

|  |  |
 --- |
|  | (2) |

where is parameterized by (empty for fixed-rule indexers; learned for trainable ones), denotes the selected index set, and denotes standard scaled dot-product softmax attention restricted to this index set. We call the first stage the Index Branch and the second the Main Branch. In multi-head attention, each query, specified by a position and a query head , can select a different key/value index set, written as ; eq.˜2 omits the head index only for notational simplicity.

### 2.3 GQA-Based Block Sparse Attention [...] ### 5.2 Training Dynamics

Figure 2 compares native sparse pretraining with the matched full-attention run. Over the 3T-token training process, the two LM-loss curves are nearly indistinguishable, showing that MSA does not introduce noticeable optimization degradation relative to full attention. The gradient-norm curves also remain within the same range throughout training, suggesting that MSA does not lead to abnormal gradient fluctuations or training instability. These results indicate that training a sparse attention model is as stable as training the full-attention baseline at a large scale.

results.title: MiniMax Sparse Attention | alphaXiv
results.content:
John: Yes, their MSA-CPT experiment, which continues pretraining from a dense GQA checkpoint, shows a viable path for adapting existing models, which is a very practical consideration for many labs.

John: To wrap up, MiniMax Sparse Attention provides a strong, practical blueprint for building and deploying LLMs with ultra-long context. It achieves massive efficiency gains by combining a simple two-branch architecture with highly optimized, hardware-aware kernels. The key takeaway here isn't just about the sparse algorithm itself, but the critical importance of algorithm-hardware co-design. Theoretical FLOP reduction is one thing, but translating that into tangible wall-clock speedup is what makes a technique truly deployable at scale.

Noah: Thanks, Professor. That makes sense. [...] 0:00 / --:--

MiniMax Sparse Attention

Transcript

John: Alright, welcome back to Transformer Architectures. Today's lecture is on the paper 'MiniMax Sparse Attention' from researchers primarily at MiniMax. We've seen a surge of interest in efficient long-context models recently, with approaches like 'Native Sparse Attention' and 'MoBA' exploring different ways to sparsify the attention mechanism. This work is significant because it tackles the same problem—the quadratic bottleneck of attention—but with a focus on a simple, deployable design that is co-designed with its GPU implementation. It’s a move towards making million-token contexts a practical reality. Yes, Noah? [...] Noah: Hi Professor. So, is the main goal here purely about efficiency, or are they also trying to improve model performance with this sparse approach?

John: That's a key point. The primary goal is efficiency without sacrificing performance. The challenge is to reduce the computational cost, which scales quadratically with sequence length, while ensuring the model remains just as capable as its full-attention counterpart across a wide range of tasks.

John: The core contribution here is a blockwise sparse attention mechanism they call MSA. It's built on Grouped Query Attention, or GQA, which is already common in many models. The architecture has two main parts: a lightweight Index Branch and a Main Branch.

Noah: So how does the Index Branch work? How does it decide what's important?

results.title: MiniMax Prepares to Launch Next-Generation M3 Large Language Model - Pandaily
results.content:
The sparse attention approach reflects a broader industry trend, with Xiaomi's MiMo team having released a similar HySparse hybrid sparse attention architecture in February 2026. The shift represents a move from purely parameter-scale competition toward efficiency and practical usability.

However, the approach faces challenges. Academic research has noted that existing sparse attention methods can lose critical information during sequence dimension compression. MiniMax will also need to address user-reported issues with the M2 series, including instruction-following instability and hallucination tendencies. [...] The M3 model's most distinctive feature is its custom sparse attention mechanism, which employs an Index Branch to rapidly scan context and identify key tokens before routing them to a Sparse Branch for precise attention computation. This design directly addresses the O(n2) computational complexity of traditional Transformers, where a 10x increase in context length results in a 100x computation increase.

According to preliminary benchmark data shared by MiniMax, the M3 achieves a 9.7x improvement in prefilling speed and a 15.6x improvement in decoding generation speed compared to the M2 model, which already supports 1 million token context windows. This translates to over 80% reduction in computing costs for enterprises processing million-token-length documents. [...] Pandaily

MiniMax Prepares to Launch Next-Generation M3 Large Language Model

# MiniMax Prepares to Launch Next-Generation M3 Large Language Model

Published:
May 28, 2026

Reading Time:
2 min read

Want to read in a language you're more familiar with?

Chinese AI unicorn MiniMax is preparing to launch its M3 large language model featuring a custom sparse attention mechanism, claiming 9.7x prefilling speed improvements.

Chinese AI unicorn MiniMax has confirmed that its next-generation large language model, M3, is entering the final preparation stage for release. The announcement came via social media posts from MiniMax AI Engineering Lead Skyler Miao, signaling a significant architectural overhaul.

results.title: MiniMax teases upcoming M3 model with new sparse attention mechanism and 15.6X long-context response speed boost | VentureBeat
results.content:
However, recognizing that physical hardware limits cannot sustain quadratic scaling indefinitely, MiniMax is designing the M3 series around a novel sub-quadratic framework to finally deliver both high-speed processing and uncompromised reasoning.

## MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) and sub-quadratic scaling incoming

The upcoming MiniMax-M3 breaks away from the compute-heavy constraints of its predecessor. As disclosed by MiniMax’s engineering team under the banner "Something BIG is coming," M3 introduces "MiniMax Sparse Attention" (MSA).

Unlike DeepSeek’s Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA), which compresses keys and values into a low-dimensional latent space, MSA operates on a standard GQA backbone but utilizes block-level selection on real, uncompressed Key-Values. [...] Now, MiniMax is again raising the eyebrows of AI power users and developers around the world by releasing a new, in-depth technical reporton the making of its popular M2 series of language models (M2, M2.5, and M2.7) shedding light on its numerous engineering innovations and clever approaches — while the company and its leaders also teased a whole new sparse attention approach for its upcoming MiniMax M3 series of models, which it says yields up to 15.6 times faster decoding (or LLM response) speed at long contexts (a million tokens) by adopting a custom sub-quadratic framework. In so doing, MiniMax has designed M3 to make ultra-long-context AI agent deployment economically viable. [...] # MiniMax teases upcoming M3 model with new sparse attention mechanism and 15.6X long-context response speed boost | VentureBeat



Among the many Chinese AI companies and laboratories vying for market share and attention (no pun intended) on the global marketplace, MiniMax stands out for its commitment to providing frontier-level intelligence across a range of modalities, including text, coding, and video (through its Hailuo model series) — often under permissive, enterprise-friendly, standard open source licenses.

results.title: Thoughts on MiniMax M3's MSA (MiniMax Sparse Attention)?
results.content:
The main thing: they built a new sparse attention mechanism called MSA (MiniMax Sparse Attention) specifically to make 1M token context viable without the usual quadratic compute explosion. The numbers they're claiming are wild if accurate - 1/20th the per-token compute of their previous gen at 1M context, 9x faster prefilling, 15x faster decoding. For local inference that matters a lot more than it does for API usage since you're actually the one paying the VRAM and compute bill. [...] How it works is they partition the KV cache into blocks and use a KV-outer-gather-Q approach instead of the usual query-centric sparse attention. Each block gets read once, memory access stays contiguous. They claim it matches full attention on most capabilities across ablations which if true is the important part - sparse attention that degrades quality isn't useful. [...] On benchmarks it's sitting at 59% on SWE-Bench Pro (above GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro, behind Opus 4.7), 91.6% on OmniDocBench which is the best in class, and leads on Claw-Eval for autonomous agent tasks. The one that actually impressed us was the CUDA kernel optimization test - they gave it an FP8 GEMM task with no reference implementation, it ran for 24 hours, made 147 benchmark submissions, and went from 7.6% to 71.3% hardware utilization. Most models gave up by submission 30. That's not a benchmark number, that's a real engineering task.

It's natively multimodal from pretraining step 0, not a adapter bolted on after. Trained on ~100 trillion tokens with interleaved text/image/video data. Also supports thinking on/off at inference time which is useful depending on your use case.

Sparse attention lets long-context models avoid reading every past token. This video explains the core

  then run full attention on that smaller set.

results.title: MiniMax Goes Sparse: Decoding M3's Attention from a Single Diagram
results.content:
Hugging Face's logo

# MiniMax Goes Sparse: Decoding M3's Attention from a Single Diagram

Atlas Cloud's avatar

 1. What the diagram actually shows: two stages, pick before you compute
  + Step 1: Index Branch — score everything cheaply
  + Step 2: Sparse Branch — where attention actually runs
 2. Three deliberate subtractions relative to the DeepSeek family
 3. How to read the numbers
 4. Inferring the rest of M3
 5. Placing M3 in the 2026 design space
 Closing thoughts
 References

On May 26, MiniMax R&D lead Skyler Miao posted a diagram on X — restrained palette, but a lot of information packed in. The title reads MiniMax Sparse Attention, and the two curves on the right give an eye-catching pair of numbers: 9.7× prefill and 15.6× decode speedup at 1M tokens. [...] Most likely natively trained sparsity. This is the central message of the NSA paper — the sparse pattern must enter gradients during pretraining, or retrieval heads get scrambled. MiniMax has its own research line on retrieval heads, so they shouldn't fall into this trap.

The battleground is 1M+ context. M1 was trained at 1M and extrapolates to 4M at inference; M3 is locking that in and slashing inference cost — a very natural product cadence.

## 5. Placing M3 in the 2026 design space

Across 2025–2026, sparse-attention designs have diverged quickly: [...] S=BlockMaxPool(QidxKidx⊤),I=TopK(S)S = \text{BlockMaxPool}(Q\_{idx} K\_{idx}^{\top}), \qquad I = \text{TopK}(S)S=BlockMaxPool(Qidx​Kidx⊤​),I=TopK(S)

Finally, TopK decides which KV blocks to keep for this layer and this GQA group; the result is `I₁`, `I₂`.

`I₁`
`I₂`

### Step 2: Sparse Branch — where attention actually runs

The bottom half is where the real attention computation happens. `Q ∈ ℝⁿˣᴴˣᵈ`, `K, V ∈ ℝⁿˣʰˣᵈ`, still in standard GQA form. Using `I₁`, `I₂` from Step 1 as indices, we pull the corresponding block subsets out of the original K/V and run:

`K, V ∈ ℝⁿˣʰˣᵈ`
